speaker: PRINCESS
text: Was that the King that spurred his horse so hard

speaker: PRINCESS
text: Against the steep uprising of the hill?

speaker: FORESTER
text: I know not, but I think it was not he.

speaker: PRINCESS
text: Whoe’er he was, he showed a mounting mind.—

speaker: PRINCESS
text: Well, lords, today we shall have our dispatch.

speaker: PRINCESS
text: Or Saturday we will return to France.—

speaker: PRINCESS
text: Then, forester, my friend, where is the bush

speaker: PRINCESS
text: That we must stand and play the murderer in?

speaker: FORESTER
text: Hereby, upon the edge of yonder coppice,

speaker: FORESTER
text: A stand where you may make the fairest shoot.

speaker: PRINCESS
text: I thank my beauty, I am fair that shoot,

speaker: PRINCESS
text: And thereupon thou speakst “the fairest shoot.”

speaker: FORESTER
text: Pardon me, madam, for I meant not so.

speaker: PRINCESS
text: What, what? First praise me, and again say no?

speaker: PRINCESS
text: O short-lived pride. Not fair? Alack, for woe!

speaker: FORESTER
text: Yes, madam, fair.

speaker: PRINCESS
text: Nay, never paint me now.

speaker: PRINCESS
text: Where fair is not, praise cannot mend the brow.

speaker: PRINCESS
text: Here, good my glass, take this for telling true.

speaker: PRINCESS
text: Fair payment for foul words is more than due.

speaker: FORESTER
text: Nothing but fair is that which you inherit.

speaker: PRINCESS
text: See, see, my beauty will be saved by merit.

speaker: PRINCESS
text: O heresy in fair, fit for these days!

speaker: PRINCESS
text: A giving hand, though foul, shall have fair praise.

speaker: PRINCESS
text:
But come, the bow.
                 
                He
                 
                hands
                 
                her
                 
                a
                 
                bow
                .
               Now mercy goes to kill,

speaker: PRINCESS
text: And shooting well is then accounted ill.

speaker: PRINCESS
text: Thus will I save my credit in the shoot:

speaker: PRINCESS
text: Not wounding, pity would not let me do ’t;

speaker: PRINCESS
text: If wounding, then it was to show my skill,

speaker: PRINCESS
text: That more for praise than purpose meant to kill.

speaker: PRINCESS
text: And out of question so it is sometimes:

speaker: PRINCESS
text: Glory grows guilty of detested crimes,

speaker: PRINCESS
text: When for fame’s sake, for praise, an outward part,

speaker: PRINCESS
text: We bend to that the working of the heart;

speaker: PRINCESS
text: As I for praise alone now seek to spill

speaker: PRINCESS
text: The poor deer’s blood, that my heart means no ill.

speaker: BOYET
text: Do not curst wives hold that self sovereignty

speaker: BOYET
text: Only for praise’ sake when they strive to be

speaker: BOYET
text: Lords o’er their lords?

speaker: PRINCESS
text: Only for praise; and praise we may afford

speaker: PRINCESS
text: To any lady that subdues a lord.

speaker: BOYET
text: Here comes a member of the commonwealth.

speaker: COSTARD
text: God dig-you-den all! Pray you, which is the

speaker: COSTARD
text: head lady?

speaker: PRINCESS
text: Thou shalt know her, fellow, by the rest that

speaker: PRINCESS
text: have no heads.

speaker: COSTARD
text: Which is the greatest lady, the highest?

speaker: PRINCESS
text: The thickest and the tallest.

speaker: COSTARD
text: The thickest and the tallest: it is so, truth is truth.

speaker: COSTARD
text: An your waist, mistress, were as slender as my wit,

speaker: COSTARD
text: One o’ these maids’ girdles for your waist should be fit.

speaker: COSTARD
text: Are not you the chief woman? You are the thickest here.

speaker: PRINCESS
text: What’s your will, sir? What’s your will?

speaker: COSTARD
text: I have a letter from Monsieur Berowne to

speaker: COSTARD
text: one Lady Rosaline.

speaker: PRINCESS
text: O, thy letter, thy letter! He’s a good friend of mine.

speaker: PRINCESS
text: Stand aside, good bearer.—Boyet, you can carve.

speaker: PRINCESS
text: Break up this capon.

speaker: BOYET
text: I am bound to serve.

speaker: BOYET
text: This letter is mistook; it importeth none here.

speaker: BOYET
text: It is writ to Jaquenetta.

speaker: PRINCESS
text: We will read it, I swear.

speaker: PRINCESS
text: Break the neck of the wax, and everyone give ear.

speaker: BOYET
text: Thus dost thou hear the Nemean lion roar

speaker: BOYET
text: ’Gainst thee, thou lamb, that standest as his prey.

speaker: BOYET
text: Submissive fall his princely feet before,

speaker: BOYET
text: And he from forage will incline to play.

speaker: BOYET
text: But if thou strive, poor soul, what art thou then?

speaker: BOYET
text: Food for his rage, repasture for his den.

speaker: PRINCESS
text: What plume of feathers is he that indited this letter?

speaker: PRINCESS
text: What vane? What weathercock? Did you ever hear better?

speaker: BOYET
text: I am much deceived but I remember the style.

speaker: PRINCESS
text: Else your memory is bad, going o’er it erewhile.

speaker: BOYET
text: This Armado is a Spaniard that keeps here in court,

speaker: BOYET
text: A phantasime, a Monarcho, and one that makes sport

speaker: BOYET
text: To the Prince and his bookmates.

speaker: PRINCESS
text: Thou, fellow, a word.

speaker: PRINCESS
text: Who gave thee this letter?

speaker: COSTARD
text: I told you: my lord.

speaker: PRINCESS
text: To whom shouldst thou give it?

speaker: COSTARD
text: From my lord to my lady.

speaker: PRINCESS
text: From which lord to which lady?

speaker: COSTARD
text: From my Lord Berowne, a good master of mine,

speaker: COSTARD
text: To a lady of France that he called Rosaline.

speaker: PRINCESS
text: Thou hast mistaken his letter. Come, lords, away.

speaker: PRINCESS
text:
To
                 
                Rosaline
                .
                 
              Here, sweet, put up this; ’twill be thine another day.

speaker: BOYET
text: Who is the shooter? Who is the shooter?

speaker: ROSALINE
text: Shall I teach you to know?

speaker: BOYET
text: Ay, my continent of beauty.

speaker: ROSALINE
text: Why, she that bears the bow.

speaker: ROSALINE
text: Finely put off.

speaker: BOYET
text: My lady goes to kill horns, but if thou marry,

speaker: BOYET
text: Hang me by the neck if horns that year miscarry.

speaker: BOYET
text: Finely put on.

speaker: ROSALINE
text: Well, then, I am the shooter.

speaker: BOYET
text: And who is your deer?

speaker: ROSALINE
text: If we choose by the horns, yourself come not near.

speaker: ROSALINE
text: Finely put on, indeed.

speaker: MARIA
text: You still wrangle with her, Boyet, and she strikes at the brow.

speaker: BOYET
text: But she herself is hit lower. Have I hit her now?

speaker: ROSALINE
text: Shall I come upon thee with an old saying,

speaker: ROSALINE
text: that was a man when King Pippen of France was a

speaker: ROSALINE
text: little boy, as touching the hit it?

speaker: BOYET
text: So I may answer thee with one as old, that was a

speaker: BOYET
text: woman when Queen Guinover of Britain was a little

speaker: BOYET
text: wench, as touching the hit it.

speaker: ROSALINE
text: Thou canst not hit it, hit it, hit it,

speaker: ROSALINE
text: Thou canst not hit it, my good man.

speaker: BOYET
text: An I cannot, cannot, cannot,

speaker: BOYET
text: An I cannot, another can.

speaker: COSTARD
text: By my troth, most pleasant. How both did fit it!

speaker: MARIA
text: A mark marvelous well shot, for they both did hit it.

speaker: BOYET
text: A mark! O, mark but that mark. “A mark,” says my lady.

speaker: BOYET
text: Let the mark have a prick in ’t to mete at, if it may be.

speaker: MARIA
text: Wide o’ the bow hand! I’ faith, your hand is out.

speaker: COSTARD
text: Indeed, he must shoot nearer, or he’ll ne’er hit the clout.

speaker: BOYET
text: An if my hand be out, then belike your hand is in.

speaker: COSTARD
text: Then will she get the upshoot by cleaving the pin.

speaker: MARIA
text: Come, come, you talk greasily. Your lips grow foul.

speaker: COSTARD
text: She’s too hard for you at pricks, sir. Challenge her to bowl.

speaker: BOYET
text: I fear too much rubbing. Good night, my good owl.

speaker: COSTARD
text: By my soul, a swain, a most simple clown.

speaker: COSTARD
text: Lord, Lord, how the ladies and I have put him down.

speaker: COSTARD
text: O’ my troth, most sweet jests, most incony vulgar wit,

speaker: COSTARD
text: When it comes so smoothly off, so obscenely, as it were, so fit.

speaker: COSTARD
text: Armado o’ th’ one side, O, a most dainty man!

speaker: COSTARD
text: To see him walk before a lady and to bear her fan.

speaker: COSTARD
text: To see him kiss his hand, and how most sweetly he will swear.

speaker: COSTARD
text: And his page o’ t’ other side, that handful of wit!

speaker: COSTARD
text: Ah heavens, it is a most pathetical nit.

speaker: COSTARD
text: Sola, sola!
